It is important to note that many other diseases can present with similar symptoms, so these alone do not confirm HIV infection. An HIV infection is formally classified as AIDS when the individual is HIV-positive and exhibits signs of advanced immune system breakdown, typically identified by the presence of a definite opportunistic disease or extremely low T-cell counts. Without powerful antiviral medications, which now significantly extend life, common infections in AIDS patients included fungal and yeast infections of the lungs and esophagus, as well as other bacterial lung infections, such as Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PCP).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 63)

title: Dementia
content: Dementia is defined as a decrease in cognitive abilities. This condition can manifest either as a chronic, slowly progressing disorder or as a component of an acute illness, such as encephalitis.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 63)

title: HIV/AIDS: Common Systemic Symptoms and Indicator Diseases
content:
Individuals with advanced HIV infection (AIDS) often experience a range of severe infections and complications due to a compromised immune system. These include fungal and yeast infections of the lungs and esophagus, as well as other bacterial lung infections, which may occur simultaneously. Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PCP), formerly known as Pneumocystis carinii, is a specific form of pneumonia affecting persons with AIDS, characterized by high fever, a severe hacking cough from fluid in the lungs, and typically necessitates hospitalization.

Swollen lymph nodes are a common symptom in individuals with HIV, indicating the immune system's struggle to fight off infection, filtering toxins and waste, and potentially a large number of dead T cells killed by HIV. Furthermore, various forms of cancer are significantly more likely to occur in persons with AIDS because the immune system can no longer adequately protect the body.

Recurrent infections are also a key symptom; a simple infection might be treated with antibiotics but quickly reoccurs. In untreated or advanced cases of HIV, dementia can result from an infection of the brain or cerebrospinal tissues, potentially leading to personality disorders or other mental illnesses. While these diseases can occur in healthy individuals, their presence in someone with HIV often indicates AIDS, earning them the designation 'AIDS-indicator diseases.'
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: Chronic Wasting Syndrome
content: Chronic wasting syndrome refers to rapid, unexplained weight loss. This condition was frequently observed in the early stages of the AIDS epidemic. However, with the advent of current antiviral therapy, it is no longer a common symptom.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: Viral Load
content: Viral load is a measurement of how many viral particles are present in a person’s blood. New tests have been developed specifically to measure viral load, which provides an indication of the actual number of viral particles in an infected person’s blood. A high viral load suggests that the person is most likely to experience symptoms.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: Enzyme Inhibitors (in HIV Treatment)
content: Enzyme inhibitors are chemicals designed to interfere with enzyme activity. In the context of HIV infection treatment, these inhibitors specifically target and interfere with the enzymes that are crucial for viral replication.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: Drug Cocktails (in HIV Treatment)
content: Drug cocktails are mixtures comprising several different drugs that are chosen to work effectively together. In the treatment of HIV infection, these combinations of medications are used to manage the virus.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: HIV/AIDS Treatment and Management Principles
content: While there is currently no cure for AIDS, substantial progress has been made in understanding the biochemistry and physical aspects of the virus, as well as its interaction with the immune system. Significant advancements have also occurred in the management of individuals living with HIV. It is crucial for persons with HIV to undergo regular testing to continuously monitor their immune systems. This constant monitoring is vital as it enables doctors to slow the progression of the disease. Modern management strategies include new tests that measure viral load, which indicates the number of viral particles in the infected person’s blood, guiding treatment decisions.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 64)

title: HIV/AIDS Monitoring and Treatment
content: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is monitored through tests measuring viral load (the number of viral particles in an infected person's blood), which indicates symptom likelihood and potential for virus spread. Another key indicator is the count of CD4 cells, a specific type of T cell severely affected by HIV; a low CD4 count signifies low resistance to diseases. To restore or partially restore the immune system in persons with HIV, scientists developed enzyme inhibitors (e.g., Azidothymidine (AZT)). These drugs interfere with the normal activity and replication process of the HIV virus, reducing its presence in the body and allowing immune function restoration. Combinations of these drugs, known as drug cocktails, interfere with various biochemical reactions essential for viral replication. This approach helps doctors keep viral loads low and CD4 counts high, enabling individuals with HIV to live longer, more productive lives with a higher quality of life. There are indications that with proper monitoring, individuals with HIV may achieve normal life spans. However, these drugs can have side effects, including intestinal symptoms like diarrhea and tiredness, and are often very expensive. A significant challenge is that the virus mutates frequently, leading to different strains of HIV, and can adapt to drug therapy, causing drugs to become less effective or stop working. Approximately 20 percent of persons with AIDS have been infected with viruses mutated from those treated with enzyme inhibitors, indicating potential transmission from treated individuals. When drugs become ineffective, immune suppression can recur, leaving the person susceptible to infections; however, doctors can often adjust drug combinations to try new approaches. Recent research by scientists at Scripps Research Institute in California has led to the discovery of two "broadly neutralizing antibodies" capable of stopping the actions of the HIV virus.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 65)

title: Abstinence
content: Abstinence means that individuals choose not to engage in sexual intercourse at all. This is a method of preventing the transmission of diseases, particularly those spread through sexual contact.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 65)

title: HIV/AIDS Prevention and the Esthetician's Role
content:
Prevention remains the most effective strategy for AIDS. This involves taking measures to prevent individuals from becoming infected with HIV. Education is crucial in preventing the spread of the virus, and estheticians, along with all health and personal care professionals, play an important role. By being knowledgeable about AIDS, estheticians can help clients understand the syndrome and answer questions regarding its prevention.

**HIV Transmission:**
HIV infection or AIDS can be transmitted in two primary ways, both involving the exchange of blood, blood products, or body fluids contaminated with HIV between individuals. Sexual contact is the leading cause of AIDS transmission.

**Prevention Techniques:**
Precautions must be diligently followed to prevent the exchange of infectious fluids:
*   **Abstinence:** The safest prevention technique is complete abstinence from sexual intercourse.
*   **Condom Use:** For those who choose to engage in sexual intercourse, using a synthetic condom is essential. Lambskin condoms are permeable and may not prevent the transmission of semen and infection, unlike synthetic condoms. Condoms must be worn consistently throughout sexual relations.
*   **Avoiding Body Penetration:** Avoiding penetration of any body orifice (such as the vagina, rectum, or mouth) significantly reduces the risk of disease transmission.
*   **Monogamy and HIV Testing:** Monogamy, where two partners are faithful and have sex only with each other, is a key preventative measure. In a monogamous relationship, if both individuals have tested negative for HIV and received medical counseling on proper HIV testing, concern about HIV infection is minimized, provided no other risk factors exist.
*   **Avoiding Intravenous Drugs:** Use of intravenous drugs is another significant risk factor for HIV transmission.

**Current Research:**
Recent scientific discoveries at Scripps Research Institute in California have identified two 'broadly neutralizing antibodies' that can block the actions of the HIV virus. These antibodies were found in blood samples of HIV-positive individuals who had not developed AIDS-related diseases, suggesting their ability to block HIV activity. This discovery holds promise for the future development of a practical HIV vaccine.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 65)

title: HIV/AIDS: Risk Factors, Complacency, and Shifting Infection Rates
content: HIV infection is primarily associated with specific risk factors. Intravenous drug use, involving the injection of illegal drugs with contaminated needles, is a significant risk factor, as addicts often share dirty needles leading to direct injection of HIV-infected blood. Sexual transmission is also a major concern, particularly with unprotected sex and multiple partners; anal and oral sex carry high risks due to potential exposure to blood or semen. A new concern is complacency stemming from successful drug therapies, leading some individuals to become careless about safe sex and needle practices, mistakenly believing AIDS is controllable. While HIV infection among homosexual males has declined, infection rates are increasing among women, teenagers, African Americans, and Hispanics.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Pages 65-66)

title: Personal Precautions for HIV/AIDS Prevention
content:
HIV infection and AIDS are preventable. Key prevention tips include: 
1.  **Safe Sexual Practices:** Always use a synthetic condom from start to finish during sexual activity. 
2.  **Avoid Multiple Partners:** Sex with multiple partners is extremely risky, as is anal and oral sex, exposing individuals to blood or semen. 
3.  **Avoid Intravenous Drugs:** Do not use intravenous drugs or engage in sexual relations with anyone who does, as AIDS is prevalent among IV drug users, many of whom are also involved in prostitution. 
4.  **No Assumptions:** Never assume that an individual is not HIV-positive; persons may carry HIV and appear completely healthy. 
5.  **Window Phase Awareness:** Be aware that even if a potential sexual partner tests negative for HIV, there is a 'window phase' during which antibodies to the virus may not have developed yet. All HIV tests detect antibodies in the blood; a person in this window phase may test negative but still carry the virus. Therefore, consistent condom use is essential.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 66)

title: Dispelling Myths About HIV/AIDS Transmission
content:
Fear and misunderstanding surrounding life-threatening diseases like AIDS often lead to rumors. It's crucial to understand the facts about how AIDS spreads: 
1.  **Primary Transmission Route:** The only way to acquire AIDS is through the exchange of blood or body fluids contaminated with blood from a person infected with HIV. 
2.  **No Casual Contact Transmission:** HIV cannot be transmitted through casual contact, such as sitting next to an infected person. 
3.  **No Sharing Utensils Transmission:** You cannot get AIDS by drinking from someone’s glass or sharing eating utensils. 
4.  **No Social Contact Transmission:** Hugging, touching, social kissing (on the cheek), or eating food prepared by an infected person do not transmit AIDS. 
5.  **No Insect Transmission:** HIV is not transmitted by mosquitoes or other insects.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 66)

title: HIV/AIDS Transmission Misconceptions
content: It is crucial for estheticians to understand and communicate accurate information regarding HIV/AIDS transmission. HIV is not transmitted through casual contact such as sharing eating utensils, drinking from someone's glass, hugging, touching, social kissing (on the cheek), or eating food prepared by an infected person. It also cannot be transmitted by mosquito or insect bites, or through French kissing. HIV is primarily transmitted through unsafe sex or sharing needles with an infected person. AIDS is not specific to any group; anyone can become infected.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 67)

title: HIV/AIDS Precautions in Esthetics Practice
content: While exposure to HIV in the cosmetology workplace has not been documented, it is extremely important for estheticians to use stringent hygiene and aseptic techniques. Procedures such as extraction, waxing, tweezing, and electrolysis may sometimes result in very small amounts of blood surfacing on the skin. Although estheticians do not typically work with body fluids in the same manner as healthcare workers, the potential for exposure to minute amounts of blood necessitates strict adherence to disinfection guidelines for all utensils and equipment. This ensures client safety and addresses concerns about preventing HIV and other disease transmission.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 67)

title: Dermatological Manifestations of HIV/AIDS
content: Early detection and medical management of HIV have reduced the likelihood of severe skin problems associated with immune system dysfunction. However, various dermatological symptoms can still occur in individuals with HIV/AIDS. These may include unusual or severe cases of common diseases such as shingles (caused by the *Herpes zoster* virus, which also causes chickenpox), herpes simplex, genital herpes, genital warts, seborrheic dermatitis, unexplained rashes, warts, impetigo, fungal infections, folliculitis, and athlete’s foot, as well as infections of the mouth and throat. While most people presenting with these infections do not have HIV, in HIV-positive individuals, such infections are often recurrent, extremely severe, or resistant to normal treatment.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 67)

title: Recognizing Potential HIV/AIDS Skin Manifestations and Esthetician's Role
content: Infections such as fungal infections, folliculitis, and athlete’s foot, as well as infections of the mouth and throat, may present as recurrent, extremely severe, or resistant to normal treatment in individuals with HIV. Because the skin is a major organ of the immune system, some of the first symptoms of HIV infection or AIDS can appear on the skin, often leading to diagnosis in a dermatologist’s office. Estheticians must be aware of these possible symptoms to encourage clients who show signs of these illnesses to see a physician, regardless of their HIV status. It is crucial to remember that these symptoms can also occur in normal, healthy, HIV-negative individuals. The esthetician must never mention the AIDS relationship of these symptoms to a client, but should only refer such problems to a doctor. Physicians may need to use more aggressive therapies to treat skin problems in persons with AIDS due to the severity and chronic nature of symptoms in this population. These symptoms may also affect mucous membranes (e.g., inside of the mouth, rectum, vagina, and eye).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Pages 67-68)

title: Herpes Viruses and Their Manifestations
content: Herpes refers to a group of viruses, with eight forms known to infect humans. Common manifestations include cold sores (Herpes Simplex Virus type 1) and genital herpes (Herpes Simplex Virus type 2). The Varicella Zoster Virus (VZV), another form of herpes, causes chickenpox and can reactivate later in life as shingles (herpes zoster), often when the immune system is deficient or in older people. Shingles lesions appear as multiple red blisters. In individuals with HIV, herpes flare-ups tend to be significantly more severe and frequent. Physicians may prescribe antiviral medications to help the suppressed immune system heal the lesions associated with a herpes infection. Not all herpes viruses are transmitted by sexual contact (as shown in Figure 4-4).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 68)

title: Warts (Verruca)
content: Warts, also known as verruca, are caused by a variety of viruses referred to as papovavirus.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 68)

title: Molluscum Contagiosum
content: Molluscum contagiosum is a viral infection frequently observed on the faces of children, appearing as groups of small, flesh-colored papules.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 68)

title: Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
content: Shingles is an adult manifestation of chickenpox, caused by the reactivation of the latent virus (Herpes Zoster) that originally caused chickenpox. The virus can lie dormant in nerve cells and later emerge as shingles, often when the immune system is deficient. This commonly occurs in older individuals with weakened immune systems, chemotherapy patients, or any person with a compromised immune system. Shingles can be very painful, and in patients with HIV, it is often much worse and more widespread (Figure 4-5).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Warts
content: Warts are the manifestation of a virus on the skin surface. They are often recurrent and more widespread in HIV-positive individuals, potentially appearing in unusual locations such as the beard area, mouth, and tongue. Plantar warts, found on the feet or hands, are the most common type of wart on the feet for all humans, regardless of HIV status. In HIV-positive patients, these warts may be resistant to normal treatment. Venereal warts, located on the genitals, anus, and vagina, are also frequently observed and are more prevalent in HIV-positive persons.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Molluscum Contagiosum
content: Molluscum contagiosum is a viral skin condition often seen in normal children, typically presenting as a large group of wart-like lesions, frequently on the face. While it was previously common in patients with advanced HIV disease, its prevalence has decreased due to the widespread use of drug cocktails.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Impetigo
content: Impetigo is an extremely contagious bacterial skin infection characterized by large, open, weeping lesions. Often seen in normal children, it can also affect adults, and in persons with AIDS, the lesions can be much worse. Impetigo presents as multiple scab-like lesions, frequently around the mouth and chin area. These lesions have a crusty top but ooze a transparent fluid. The condition is caused by a bacterium normally present in the nares (nostrils) that overgrows when the immune system is compromised. Clients exhibiting these symptoms should be referred to a physician immediately.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Mycosis
content: Mycosis refers to fungal infections. Many fungal infections can affect both the skin and the internal organs of a person, particularly those with AIDS. In individuals with AIDS, the presence of skin infections from fungi may indicate underlying internal fungal infections. Fungal infections can manifest with many different forms of symptomatic lesions.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Folliculitis
content: Folliculitis is a bacterial infection of the hair follicle. It is caused by an irritation of the hair follicle that subsequently develops into a bacterial infection. The affected follicles appear inflamed, often in groups or patches, and may manifest as patches of very tiny pustules. Persons with curly hair are more susceptible to folliculitis due to the tendency of curly hair to become trapped or irritated within the follicle.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Seborrheic Dermatitis (Seborrhea)
content: Seborrheic dermatitis, also known as seborrhea, is an inflammation of the sebaceous glands and follicles. This condition results in redness, flaking, and itching, primarily affecting the oily areas of the face.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 69)

title: Folliculitis
content: Folliculitis is caused by an irritation of the hair follicle that develops into a bacterial infection. The affected follicles appear inflamed, often in groups or patches, and may manifest as patches of very tiny pustules. Individuals with curly hair are more susceptible to folliculitis due to the tendency of curly hair to become ingrown, which can cause follicle irritation.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Severe Seborrheic Dermatitis in HIV/AIDS Patients
content: Severe seborrheic dermatitis is commonly observed in persons with HIV/AIDS. While many healthy individuals may experience small areas of seborrheic dermatitis, in those with AIDS, the condition is significantly worse, more widespread, inflamed, and chronic. Symptoms include red, scaly patches apparent across all areas of the face, particularly the hairline and ears, and can extend to the scalp. Severe cases may lead to cracks in the skin, bleeding, and oozing lesions (Figure 4-6). Standard treatments are often ineffective, requiring more potent topical steroids to manage the disorder.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Psoriasis in HIV/AIDS Patients
content: Psoriasis, an inflammatory skin condition, may develop or worsen in persons with AIDS, indicating a potential exacerbation of autoimmune or immune-mediated skin issues in an immunocompromised state.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Oral Thrush
content: Oral thrush is an infection of the mouth and throat caused by the yeast Candida albicans. It is often one of the earliest symptoms of AIDS. Symptoms include an abnormally thick, white paste covering mouth tissues, resembling cotton stuck to patches in the mouth. While it commonly affects persons with HIV and small children, it is rarely seen in normal, healthy adults.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Hairy Leukoplakia
content: Hairy leukoplakia is a tumor-like eruption that can occur on the tongue or the insides of the cheeks. These lesions are characterized by a hard, scaly appearance.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Herpes Lesions (Fever Blisters) in HIV/AIDS Patients
content: Herpes lesions, commonly known as 'fever blisters,' may affect the inside of the mouth in persons with AIDS, indicating a compromised immune response to the virus.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Kaposi's Sarcoma (KS)
content: Kaposi’s sarcoma (KS) is a rare form of skin cancer characterized by purple-brown lesions. At one time, KS was a frequent skin symptom of AIDS, affecting immunosuppressed individuals. However, due to advancements in antiviral treatment, persons with HIV are now much less likely to develop Kaposi’s sarcoma (Figure 4-7).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Pages 70-71)

title: Unusual Skin Lesions in HIV/AIDS Patients
content: Persons with HIV/AIDS frequently present with many unusual skin lesions, including strange skin bumps. Due to their lowered resistance to infection caused by immunosuppression, physicians continually observe new and atypical lesions on the skin of these individuals.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 70)

title: Skin Manifestations and Cancers in HIV/AIDS Patients
content: Individuals with HIV/AIDS may present with various skin conditions. Seborrheic dermatitis, for example, can be observed in HIV patients (as shown in Figure 4-6). Historically, Kaposi’s sarcoma (as shown in Figure 4-7) was a common skin cancer in persons with HIV; however, due to advancements in antiviral treatment, its occurrence is now much less likely. Other skin cancers, including basal cell carcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas, are detected in persons with AIDS and tend to spread much faster in these individuals. Malignant melanoma may also be observed and similarly tends to spread more rapidly in immunosuppressed individuals.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Pages 70-71)

title: Esthetician's Role: Supporting Clients with HIV/AIDS and Public Education
content: Persons with AIDS or who are HIV-positive may experience significant emotional challenges, including anger, denial, and difficulty coping due to the incurable nature of the disease and ongoing uncertainties about management. While the stigma surrounding AIDS is decreasing, public misinformation persists. Estheticians play a crucial part in helping to educate the public by tactfully correcting incorrect information from clients about AIDS. Furthermore, estheticians must be highly knowledgeable about salon disinfection and aseptic procedures to confidently and factually answer client questions regarding AIDS and salon safety. Staying well-informed is essential to dispel rumors and educate consumers effectively.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Pages 71-72)

title: Key Medical Terminology: Hepatitis, Jaundice, and Cirrhosis
content:
Understanding certain medical terms is important for a holistic view of client health: 
*   **Hepatitis**: An inflammation of the liver.
*   **Jaundice**: A yellowing of the skin and eyes, often indicative of liver issues.
*   **Cirrhosis**: Scarring within the liver caused by chronic inflammation. It can result from chronic viral infection and is frequently caused by alcoholism. Cirrhosis can lead to severe health consequences, including liver failure, liver cancer, and ultimately, death.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 72)

title: Esthetician's Role in Addressing AIDS/HIV Concerns and Maintaining Asepsis
content:
Esthetology professionals are in a unique position to educate consumers about issues like AIDS and dispel rumors. Key tips for handling questions and ensuring safety include:
1. Answer questions factually and briefly, without dwelling on the subject.
2. Reassure clients that all equipment used is disinfected or disposable, and that all precautions are taken. Displaying a poster or plaque outlining disinfection practices is recommended.
3. Maintain an immaculately clean salon or clinic. Allow clients to observe hand washing and adherence to aseptic procedures, as a clean environment helps alleviate client doubts.
4. Ensure all coworkers are equally informed about AIDS facts and aseptic/disinfecting procedures.
5. If a client confides about exposure or sickness, refer them to a physician immediately, maintaining a caring attitude.
At the time of this writing, 1 in 250 people in the United States is HIV-positive, highlighting the likelihood of knowing someone affected. Despite progress in treatment, the hope for a cure or vaccine continues.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 72)

title: Hepatitis: Causes, Symptoms, Complications, and Transmission
content:
The liver is a vital organ responsible for breaking down fats, storing energy, detoxifying red blood cells, and producing enzymes for digestion. Hepatitis is an inflammation of the liver that interferes with its proper functioning.

**Causes:**
* Viral or bacterial infection
* Alcohol or chronic drug abuse
* Autoimmune disease (rarely)
* Overuse of acetaminophen, particularly when combined with alcohol consumption

**Symptoms:**
* Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes)
* Abdominal pain
* Fever
* Constant fatigue

Hepatitis can lead to permanent damage to the liver.

**Cirrhosis:** This is scarring within the liver caused by chronic inflammation, often due to chronic viral infection or alcoholism. Cirrhosis can progress to liver failure, liver cancer, and death. Severe viral infection can directly cause liver failure, which is frequently fatal.

**Carrier:** A person who is infected with an illness, such as the hepatitis virus, and is able to transmit it to another person. A carrier may or may not show symptoms of the disease. The hepatitis virus can remain dormant in the liver even after initial symptoms subside, allowing the infected person to continue transmitting the virus. Hepatitis viruses are extremely infectious, with a significantly higher likelihood of infection compared to HIV.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Disease Carrier
content: A carrier is an individual who harbors an infectious agent (such as a virus) and can transmit it to other people, even if they do not display symptoms of the disease themselves. They remain infectious after the initial disease resolves, posing an ongoing risk of transmission.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis Viruses: Overview
content: Hepatitis refers to inflammation of the liver caused by highly infectious viruses. Hepatitis viruses (types A, B, C, D, and E) are significantly more infectious than HIV, as they are present in much higher concentrations in infected individuals. This high viral load contributes to a much greater probability of becoming infected with a hepatitis virus upon exposure.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis A (HAV)
content: Hepatitis A is contracted by ingesting food or water contaminated with the virus, typically through fecal material due to poor hygiene. A common association is with eating raw shellfish (like oysters) that have filtered water contaminated with human waste. It is generally the least dangerous type of hepatitis, with most people making a complete recovery. A vaccine for Hepatitis A is available.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis B (HBV)
content: Hepatitis B is a much more severe infection, primarily spread through sexual contact or exposure to body fluids, similar to HIV. However, unlike HIV, HBV can also be transmitted through kissing. Symptoms can persist for months. While most individuals recover fully, about 10% become chronic carriers, and some develop permanent liver damage. Long-term carriers (30+ years) are at an elevated risk of liver cancer, contributing to approximately 2 million deaths worldwide annually. A highly effective vaccine for Hepatitis B is available and strongly recommended for estheticians due to the potential for occupational exposure to bodily fluids. Prevention involves precautionary measures similar to those for HIV.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis C (HCV)
content: Hepatitis C presents symptoms similar to Hepatitis B and is occasionally linked to HIV infection. It frequently progresses into a lifelong chronic condition, which can lead to severe complications such as cirrhosis of the liver and liver cancer. Importantly, Hepatitis C has been detected in the skin and sweat of infected individuals. Currently, there is no vaccine available for Hepatitis C. Prevention requires adherence to precautionary measures comparable to those for HIV.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis D (HDV) and Hepatitis E (HEV)
content: Hepatitis D is a serious infection that exclusively affects individuals already infected with Hepatitis B. Co-infection with HDV significantly increases the likelihood of permanent liver damage. Hepatitis E is similar to Hepatitis A and is primarily observed in underdeveloped countries.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Hepatitis Prevention in Esthetics Practice
content:
Given the high infectivity of hepatitis viruses, strict prevention protocols are essential for estheticians:
*   **Vaccination:** Vaccinations are available for Hepatitis A and B (administered in a series of injections to build immunity) and are highly advisable for estheticians due to the potential for periodic exposure to blood and body fluids. There is no vaccine for Hepatitis C.
*   **General Precautions:** Hepatitis B and C prevention involves the same precautionary measures used for HIV. Hepatitis A can be prevented through proper disinfection, diligent hand washing, and adherence to general cleanliness guidelines.
*   **Salon Protocols:** Implement proper disinfection of all reusable implements, ensure proper disposal of contaminated materials, prioritize the use of single-use implements where possible, and consistently use hospital-grade disinfectants. (Further details on hygiene and infection control are provided in Chapter 2).
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 73)

title: Preventing Hepatitis Transmission in the Salon
content: Estheticians are periodically exposed to blood and body fluids, making hepatitis prevention critical. The best methods for preventing accidental exposure to hepatitis viruses in the salon include: proper disinfection of implements, proper disposal of contaminated materials, the use of single-use implements, and the use of hospital-grade disinfectants. Hepatitis viruses are technically harder to kill than HIV and are much more numerous in body fluids. Consequently, a person is much more likely to contract hepatitis rather than HIV from an improperly disinfected or contaminated implement. Strict adherence to proper disinfection procedures significantly reduces this risk.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 74)

title: Understanding HIV/AIDS Transmission and Debunking Myths
content: Any person can contract AIDS. To become infected with HIV, infected blood or body fluids must enter the body. It is important to note that HIV/AIDS is not spread by mosquitoes, casual contact, kissing, swimming pools, toilet seats, contact through cosmetology, pets, drinking fountains, food handling, or donating blood. Transmission occurs only through the exchange of blood, blood products, or specific body fluids. While promiscuous sex is a risk factor, anyone can contract HIV from unprotected sex with an infected person; it is not limited to any specific group. There are other diseases, such as hepatitis, that are much easier to transmit than AIDS/HIV.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 74)

title: Professional Guidelines and Universal Precautions for HIV/AIDS in the Salon
content: It is often impossible to tell by sight if a person has HIV or AIDS, as symptoms may not show up for years. The most effective way to ensure proper hygiene and prevent the spread of diseases in the salon is to assume that all clients are HIV-positive and implement universal precautions. HIV tests may produce false negatives if the person has not yet developed antibodies to the virus. Individuals should be tested several times over several months, avoiding risky activities during that period, for accurate results. It is against the law to discriminate against persons with AIDS or HIV, and estheticians must treat all clients with respect and adhere to non-discriminatory practices.
chapterTitle: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 4: COMMON DISEASES THAT AFFECT THE SKIN CARE PRACTICE (Page 74)

title: Introduction to Hormones and the Endocrine System for Estheticians
content:
Estheticians must be familiar with the endocrine system and hormones due to their significant and direct effects on the skin. Hormone functioning is related to many skin problems, such as acne and symptoms of aging skin related to menopause.

Hormones are chemical messengers manufactured or secreted by glands within the body. There are several types of glands:
*   **Exocrine Glands:** Have ducts through which chemical secretions move. Examples include the sebaceous glands and sudoriferous (sweat) glands.
*   **Apocrine Glands:** A type of sweat gland present in specific areas like the groin and armpit.
*   **Endocrine Glands:** These are ductless glands that secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream. While many people associate hormones with sex hormones, these are only a subset of the hormones produced by the endocrine system.

Hormones function by transmitting chemical messages to various body cells. They possess special 'keys' that fit into specific 'locks' located on the cell membranes of their target cells.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Hormones (Page 75)

title: Hormones and Cellular Receptors
content: Hormones are specialized chemicals manufactured or secreted by glands within the body. They function by transmitting chemical messages to various body cells. This process involves a 'key and lock' mechanism where hormones (keys) bind to specific receptor sites (locks) on cell membranes. This binding event triggers the cell membrane to produce enzymes, which in turn stimulate other chemical reactions within the cell. Each hormone is designed to affect specific cells in unique ways, and different endocrine glands produce distinct hormones. The 'locks' on the cells are called receptors or receptor sites.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Major Endocrine Glands of the Human Body
content: The human body contains eight major endocrine glands, collectively forming the endocrine system, as illustrated in Figure 5-1. These glands include the Pituitary, Pineal, Thyroid, Parathyroid glands, Thymus, Adrenal gland, Pancreas (specifically the Islets of Langerhans), Ovary (in females), and Testis (in males).
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Pituitary Gland: The Endocrine System's 'Brain'
content: The pituitary gland, found in the center of the head, acts as the 'brain' of the endocrine system. It is connected to the brain by another endocrine gland called the hypothalamus gland. The pituitary gland secretes many hormones known as trophic hormones, which are chemicals that stimulate other glands to produce their own hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Hypothalamus Gland: Interpreter and Communicator
content: The hypothalamus gland serves as an 'interpreter' between the brain and the pituitary gland. It can detect the needs of various body parts by chemically monitoring the blood that flows through it. The hypothalamus acts as a communicator between the endocrine system and the nervous system. It secretes neurohormones, which trigger the pituitary gland to make specific hormones, and also manufactures hormones that stimulate the pituitary gland to make other hormones. Additionally, the hypothalamus controls some involuntary muscles, such such as those in the intestines that aid in food movement through the gastrointestinal system.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Trophic Hormones
content: Trophic hormones are a class of chemicals secreted by the pituitary gland. Their primary function is to cause other endocrine glands throughout the body to manufacture and release their own specific hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Secretion (Physiology)
content: In a physiological context, 'secreted' refers to the process by which various cells or organs synthesize and/or release chemical substances, such as hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Classification of Glands: Exocrine, Endocrine, and Apocrine
content:
Glands in the human body can be broadly classified by their mode of secretion:
*   **Exocrine Glands**: These glands, such as sebaceous glands and sudoriferous (sweat) glands, possess ducts through which they release their chemicals to a specific location (e.g., the skin surface).
*   **Apocrine Glands**: These are a type of sudoriferous gland primarily located in the groin and armpit areas. They produce a thicker form of sweat that, when it comes into contact with bacteria on the skin, contributes to body odor.
*   **Endocrine Glands**: These glands secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream, allowing the hormones to travel throughout the body to reach their target cells.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 78)

title: Neurohormones
content: Neurohormones are a type of hormone secreted by the hypothalamus. Their primary function is to trigger the pituitary gland to produce specific hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Hypothalamus
content: The hypothalamus is an endocrine gland that manufactures hormones stimulating the pituitary gland to produce other hormones. It also plays a crucial role in chemically monitoring the blood to detect the needs of various parts of the body.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Trophic Hormones and Pituitary Gland Functions
content: Trophic hormones are chemicals that act as "signal hormones," causing other glands to produce their own hormones. Those secreted by the pituitary gland include follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which specifically prompts the production of sex hormones in the gonads. The pituitary gland also produces hormones that regulate fluid retention, control growth, and stimulate milk production in the female breast.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Esthetician's Role in Recognizing Hormonal Disorders
content: While estheticians do not diagnose illnesses, it is crucial to be aware of symptoms that might indicate hormonal or endocrine-related skin problems. Estheticians should be able to discuss a client’s skin symptoms related to such conditions and always refer clients with suspicious skin problems or multiple symptoms of any illness to a physician.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Thyroid Gland and Its Hormones
content: The thyroid gland, located in the neck, is vital for regulating cellular and body metabolism, and producing hormones that stimulate growth. Its two main metabolic regulatory hormones are thyroxine and triiodothyronine. The thyroid also produces calcitonin, a hormone that regulates calcium metabolism and usage within the body.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Hyperthyroidism: Symptoms and Skin Manifestations
content: Hyperthyroidism is a condition characterized by the thyroid gland secreting an excessive amount of thyroid hormone. Physical symptoms include heart palpitations, weight loss, and fatigue. Esthetic and skin symptoms often present as thinning of the skin, heat intolerance, facial flushing, hair loss, and rapidly growing nails. A specific skin symptom is myxedema, where the skin on the shins becomes thick, red-brown, and bumpy.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Hypothyroidism: Symptoms and Skin Manifestations
content: Hypothyroidism is the opposite of hyperthyroidism, occurring when the thyroid gland does not produce enough hormone. Esthetic symptoms, often associated with weight gain, poor balance, or hearing problems, include puffy eyelids, facial swelling, intolerance to cold temperatures, dry, flaky, coarse-textured and very dehydrated skin, and dry and brittle hair and nails.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Graves' Disease
content: Graves' disease is an autoimmune disease that can lead to either hyper- or hypothyroidism. Direct symptoms may include an enlarged thyroid gland (known as goiter) and bulging eyes. This condition can also be associated with vitiligo, a hypopigmentation disorder.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 79)

title: Graves' Disease
content: Graves' disease is an autoimmune condition associated with hyperthyroidism. Symptoms can include an enlarged thyroid gland (goiter) and bulging eyes. It may also be linked to the hypopigmentation disorder vitiligo.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Thyroid Gland
content: The thyroid gland is located in the neck. It plays a crucial role in regulating both cellular and overall body metabolism and produces hormones that stimulate growth. The two main hormones secreted by the thyroid gland are Thyroxine and Calcitonin.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Thyroid Hormones: Thyroxine and Calcitonin
content: Thyroxine is one of the two main hormones secreted by the thyroid gland, essential for metabolic regulation. Calcitonin is another hormone secreted by the thyroid gland, involved in calcium regulation.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Hyperthyroidism
content: Hyperthyroidism is a condition characterized by the thyroid gland secreting an excessive amount of thyroid hormone, leading to an overactive metabolism.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Hypothyroidism
content: Hypothyroidism is a condition in which the thyroid gland secretes an insufficient amount of thyroid hormone, resulting in a slowed metabolism.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Iodine for Thyroid Function
content: The thyroid gland requires a significant amount of iodine for the manufacture of its hormones. Therefore, incorporating iodine into the diet is important for proper thyroid function. Good dietary sources of iodine include fish, meat, and some vegetables.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Parathyroid Gland
content: The parathyroid gland is a glandular structure located behind the thyroid gland. Its primary function is to regulate calcium and phosphates in the bloodstream, which are essential substances for proper bone growth. Vitamin D also plays an important role in the regulation of calcium.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Adrenal Glands and Hormones
content: The adrenal glands are situated just above the kidneys. Structurally, they consist of an inner medulla and an outer cortex. The medulla produces adrenaline and noradrenaline, two hormones crucial for the nervous system's transport of nerve impulses. Adrenaline, often called the 'emergency hormone,' is secreted rapidly when the body is under stress, triggering physiological responses like increased heartbeat, dilated pupils, and expanded lung bronchi, preparing the body for an emergency and can even confer temporary superhuman strength during critical situations.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Adrenal Cortex and Corticoids
content: The cortex of the adrenal glands manufactures steroid hormones, known as corticoids. These are very small hormone molecules capable of penetrating cell membranes to enter cells for specific functions. Corticoids help regulate metabolism, including the body’s use of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. They also play a role in maintaining water balance and regulating sodium and potassium levels in the body.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Adrenal Gland Disorders and Skin Symptoms
content: Disorders of the adrenal glands can manifest with various skin symptoms. One notable condition is Cushing’s syndrome, which is an adrenal gland disorder.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Cushing's Syndrome
content: Cushing's syndrome is a disease of the adrenal glands characterized by the secretion of too much hydrocortisone. This excess hydrocortisone can be produced by the body or result from too much medicinal hydrocortisone. Skin symptoms associated with Cushing's syndrome include thinning of the skin and bruises that occur easily.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Addison's Disease
content: Addison's disease is an endocrine disorder of the adrenal glands, which is the exact opposite of Cushing's syndrome, where the adrenal glands do not produce enough adrenal hormones. Skin symptoms include severe hyperpigmentation on the face, dark freckles on the torso, and hyperpigmentation on the palms of the hands. Addison's disease is easily treated with hormone therapy.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Diabetes Mellitus
content: Diabetes is a disease that results from the pancreas not secreting enough insulin, a hormone manufactured by specialized cells called the islets of Langerhans within the pancreas. Insulin regulates blood sugar or glucose levels. Diabetics must often take synthetically produced insulin. Uncontrolled diabetes can lead to chronic or fatal heart, blood vessel, and kidney problems, and can cause or worsen many other diseases.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Type 1 Diabetes
content: Type 1 diabetes, also sometimes called juvenile diabetes, is caused by the failure of the pancreas to secrete insulin. Insulin is necessary for the body to convert dietary carbohydrates (sugars and starches) into energy for the cells and body. Less than 10 percent of persons who have diabetes have type 1 diabetes, which is controlled by the use of insulin and other drugs.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Type 2 Diabetes and Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT)
content: Type 2 diabetes, sometimes called adult-onset diabetes, is the most prevalent form of the disease. It is related to obesity and being chronically overweight, and may also involve genetic, age, and ethnic factor influences. In Type 2 diabetes, there may be a deficiency of insulin, or the cells do not respond to insulin normally, allowing blood sugar levels to increase outside the cells. This lack of response to insulin is known as impaired glucose tolerance (IGT). Type 2 diabetes can often be avoided or prevented by controlling the diet, keeping weight to a normal range, and exercising regularly.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Gestational Diabetes
content: Gestational diabetes is a form of diabetes that affects pregnant women. It is believed to be caused by hormones from the placenta, which sometimes produce an insulin resistance that results in high blood sugar. The fetus may also experience high blood sugar due to this condition.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 80)

title: Diabetes-Related Skin Conditions and Esthetician's Role
content:
Diabetes frequently causes or worsens a variety of skin conditions; the American Diabetes Association states that approximately 33 percent of all diabetic patients experience skin issues. These conditions are often significantly influenced by poor circulation and immune dysfunction associated with diabetes. It is important for estheticians to recognize that many diabetes-related skin problems fall outside their scope of practice.

**Common Skin Conditions Associated with Diabetes:**
*   **Infections:** Increased susceptibility to bacterial, yeast, and fungal infections of the skin.
*   **Diabetic dermopathy:** Characterized by brown, scaly lesions, primarily on the legs, caused by damage to small blood vessels.
*   **Acanthosis nigricans:** Presents as brown, raised areas of skin typically found on the neck, groin, and armpits.
*   **Wound healing issues:** Due to poor circulation and a compromised immune response, various forms of lesions in diabetic individuals do not heal quickly or easily. Advanced stages of diabetes, particularly affecting the feet and extremities, often necessitate specialized wound care.

**Esthetician's Role:** While not directly treating these medical conditions, estheticians can play a supportive role by encouraging and reinforcing good, consistent skin care practices to help maintain skin health and potentially prevent problems for diabetic clients. However, any suspicious lesions or unhealing wounds should always be referred to a medical professional.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Gestational Diabetes
content: Gestational diabetes is a form of diabetes that affects pregnant women. It is believed to be caused by hormones secreted from the placenta, which can sometimes lead to insulin resistance, resulting in high blood sugar levels. This condition is detrimental to both mother and baby; the fetus may also experience high blood sugar and, in response, produce elevated levels of insulin, potentially causing health and birth issues. Affecting about 4 percent of all pregnancies, it is crucial for all pregnant women to be aware of gestational diabetes.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Pancreas
content: The pancreas is an organ located in the abdomen. Its functions include secreting pancreatic enzymes that are delivered into the intestine for digestion. Crucially, the pancreas is also responsible for producing insulin; diabetes is a disease that arises when the pancreas does not secrete enough insulin to regulate blood sugar levels.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Pineal Gland
content: The pineal gland is an endocrine gland located in the brain. Its exact function is not yet well understood, but it is theorized to have a relationship with sex hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Thymus Gland
content: The thymus gland is located under the breastbone. It plays a vital role in the immune system, primarily by producing specialized lymphocytes that help the body fight disease (a topic further discussed in Chapter 3, The Immune System). The thymus gland is notable for growing throughout childhood and subsequently beginning to shrink in later years.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Ovaries (Female Sex Glands)
content: The ovaries are the primary female sex glands, situated above the uterus and connected to it by two hollow tubes known as the fallopian tubes (as illustrated in Figure 5-2, depicting female reproductive organs). The ovaries contain eggs and are responsible for producing female sex hormones, which have significant impacts throughout the body, including the skin.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 81)

title: Female Reproductive Organs
content: The female reproductive system comprises several key components, including the ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, vagina, cervix, clitoris, urethra, pubic bone, urinary bladder, anus, rectum, fimbriae, and vulva (as shown in Figure 5-2). The ovaries, located just above the uterus, are connected by two hollow fallopian tubes. They contain eggs that are eventually released into the fallopian tubes for potential fertilization by sperm.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Male Reproductive Organs
content: The male reproductive system includes the testes, which are situated within the scrotum and are responsible for producing sperm. The testes are connected to the vas deferens, a tube that leads to the seminal vesicle, a sac designed to hold sperm manufactured by the testes. This series of tubules ultimately connects with the urethra, the tube that extends to the outside of the penis (as shown in Figure 5-3).
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Testosterone and Androgens
content: Testosterone is the primary male hormone secreted by the testes. It is responsible for the development of typical male characteristics, such as a deep voice, broad shoulders, and body hair. Collectively, male hormones are referred to as androgens.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Estrogen (Estradiol)
content: Estrogen is one of the key female hormones produced by the ovaries. It is responsible for imparting female characteristics, such as breasts, and is crucial for the development of the menstrual cycle. Estradiol is a steroid derived from estrogen that contributes to these female characteristics.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Progesterone
content: Progesterone is a steroid hormone secreted by the ovaries. Its primary functions include preparing the uterus for pregnancy and playing a significant role in the menstrual cycle.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Relaxin
content: Relaxin is a hormone manufactured by the ovaries. Its main function is to help enlarge the pelvic opening during childbirth, facilitating delivery.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 82)

title: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH)
content: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a hormone secreted by the pituitary gland. It is responsible for initiating the development of the egg, or ovum, within the ovaries.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 83)

title: Luteinizing Hormone (LH)
content: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a hormone that directly causes the process of ovulation, which is the release of an egg from the ovary.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 83)

title: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH)
content: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a trophic hormone secreted by the pituitary gland. In females, it plays a crucial role in causing the development of the egg, or ovum. In males, FSH from the pituitary gland causes the testes to produce sperm.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 83)

title: Luteinizing Hormone (LH)
content: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a trophic hormone secreted by the pituitary gland. In females, LH causes the actual process of ovulation, which is the release of the egg (ovum) from the ovary. In males, LH stimulates the testes to manufacture testosterone.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 83)

title: Puberty: Hormonal Cascade and Effects on Body and Skin
content:
Puberty is a pivotal stage of life where significant physical changes occur in both sexes, marking the beginning of sexual function in the sex glands and making sexual reproduction physically possible. It typically commences around 12 to 14 years of age, though females may experience it earlier.

The onset of puberty is triggered by the hypothalamus, which starts producing luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH). LHRH, in turn, stimulates the pituitary gland to produce much larger amounts of trophic hormones: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) and Luteinizing Hormone (LH). These increased levels of FSH and LH then cause the ovaries and testes to secrete more sex hormones, specifically estrogens and androgens.

The sudden production of these hormones leads to a cascade of drastic physical changes:
*   **In females:** Development of breasts, formation of fat deposits around the hips to create feminine curvature, and the onset of sweat gland activity leading to body odor. Androgen production in females also contributes to the growth of pubic, leg, and underarm hair.
*   **In males:** Development of muscles, a more masculine form, broader shoulders, a deeper voice, growth of body and facial hair, and general physical growth.

Critically for esthetics, puberty profoundly affects the skin. As hormone production by the sex organs increases, androgen (a male hormone produced by both males and females) plays a significant role in stimulating the sebaceous glands to produce more sebum.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 83)

title: Androgen's Effects on Sebaceous Glands and Pore Visibility During Puberty
content: During puberty, the production of androgen (a male hormone present in both males and females) significantly impacts the skin. Androgen acts as a sebaceous gland stimulant, leading to increased sebum production. This increase in sebaceous activity causes dilation of the follicles, making 'pores' easily visible. Unlike small children who typically lack visible pore structure, teenagers begin to show dilated pores as sebum fills and stretches the follicle walls. The nose is usually the first area to develop visible pores, followed by the bridge of the nose, forehead, and chin. Increased androgen production also causes the scalp to become oilier. These effects contribute to the formation of what is known as the T-zone.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 84)

title: Simple Skincare Regimen for Teenagers with Puberty-Related Skin Changes
content:
Puberty often brings tendencies toward acne and breakouts, with young clients potentially presenting with large or small comedones in the T-zone (nose, chin, forehead). While it is crucial to instruct young clients on proper home care, the regimen must be kept simple, as they may not be accustomed to daily skin care. A recommended regimen includes: 
1.  **Cleansing:** Use a washable, foaming cleanser twice a day.
2.  **Toning:** Follow with a gentle, low-pH toner.
3.  **Treatment (Night):** If necessary, apply a mild anti-acne product at night, such as a 2% salicylic acid or 2.5% benzoyl peroxide gel.
4.  **Sun Protection:** Daily sunscreen use is extremely important to prevent future sun damage and instill good long-term skin health habits. 
Estheticians should be very thorough in explaining procedures and emphasize the dangers of excessive sun exposure.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 84)

title: The T-zone and Combination Skin Characteristics
content: The T-zone is a specific area of the face comprising the forehead, nose, and chin, which typically becomes oilier during puberty due to increased androgen production and sebaceous gland activity. This area is characterized by more visible and dilated pores compared to other parts of the face. The pattern of an oilier T-zone combined with smaller pores (indicating drier skin) in other areas of the face is characteristic of combination skin, as illustrated in Figure 5-4.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 84)

title: Combination Skin Identification
content: Combination skin is characterized by an oilier T-zone (the central area of the face including the forehead, nose, and chin) and drier skin in the perimeter of the face, indicated by smaller pores in those areas, as shown in Figure 5-4.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: Keratosis Pilaris
content: Keratosis pilaris is a condition often associated with puberty, in which the skin exhibits redness and irritation in patches, accompanied by a rough texture and small, pinpoint-sized white papules that resemble very small milia.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: General Skincare Recommendations for Pubescent Teenagers
content: Estheticians should teach good health habits at a young age, emphasizing the dangers of excessive sun exposure and the consistent use of sunscreens to prevent future sun damage and help clients maintain good skin for a lifetime. For most pubescent teenagers, moisturizers are rarely needed, except in very cold climates. Always recommend products that are noncomedogenic (refer to Chapter 15, Acne and the Esthetician, and Chapter 16, Comedogenicity for further details) to avoid clogging pores and promoting breakouts.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: Professional Facial Treatment Protocol for Teenage Clients (Oily/Combination/Comedone-Prone Skin)
content:
A professional facial treatment for teenage clients, particularly those with oily or combination skin and comedones, should follow these steps:
1.  **Cleansing**: Cleanse the face thoroughly with a cleansing milk designed for oily or combination skin. Avoid using toner or astringent at this initial stage, as the follicles are often already tight, which can make extractions more difficult.
2.  **Pre-softening**: Pre-soften clogged areas by applying a desincrustant solution or pre-mask. Steam the face with this solution for approximately 8 minutes, ensuring the skin is well-hydrated.
3.  **Preparation for Extraction**: Remove the pre-mask or desincrustant solution and gently pat the skin dry.
4.  **Extraction**: Begin extractions gently, explaining each step to the young client. Emphasize that extractions should always be performed by a professional. Be gentle and patient, as tightly packed comedones can be challenging. Apply slow, gentle pressure in small areas.
5.  **Post-Extraction Toning**: After extraction is complete, apply a toner or antiseptic astringent.
6.  **Hydration & High Frequency**: Apply a light hydrating fluid, followed by high-frequency current.
7.  **Mask**: Apply a clay-based drying mask, suitable for oily skin, to conclude the treatment.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: Educational Focus in First Facial Treatments for Teenagers
content: The initial facial treatment for a teenage client should be an educational experience rather than solely a 'feel-good' treatment. Estheticians should help young clients understand, in simple terms, what is happening to their skin and the importance of consistent hygiene. It is crucial to explain the benefits of noncomedogenic cosmetics and the necessity of professional advice when choosing the right products. Additionally, educate teenage clients about acne development so they are aware of possible future occurrences and how to prevent them. If the young client already experiences acne flare-ups, provide appropriate treatment recommendations (refer to Chapter 15, Acne and the Esthetician).
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: Recommended Frequency for Teenage Facial Treatments
content: For most pubescent teenagers, professional facial treatments are typically necessary only bimonthly. This frequency may need to be adjusted based on the client's further development of pore structure or the beginning of more frequent acne flare-ups.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 85)

title: Acne Treatment Frequency in Pubescent Teens and Adolescents
content: For most pubescent teens, bimonthly treatments for acne may be necessary until further development of the pore structure or the beginning of acne flare-ups occurs. As teenagers progress through their adolescent years, they typically experience more problems with acne. Salon treatments for these clients should then be administered on at least a monthly basis, except in cases of more severe acne, which may require more frequent treatments.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 86)

title: Keratosis Pilaris: Description, Cause, and Esthetic Treatment
content: Keratosis Pilaris is a condition that appears as small, pinpoint-sized bumps, typically on the cheeks of "rosy-cheeked" children, accompanied by generalized redness. This condition, which also affects adults (primarily on the upper arms), is frequently seen in the skin care salon. It is caused by androgens affecting the growth of either terminal or lanugo hairs. These hairs start growing but are not strong enough to push through the follicle opening, remaining trapped inside the follicle and irritating it and the surrounding skin. Treatment should be very gentle, utilizing mildly abrasive scrubs, light enzyme gommage treatments, and light extraction. Routine use of a mild abrasive, such as granular scrubs that are not excessively drying, helps to open the follicle and allow the hair to emerge, thereby reducing irritation. Additionally, a 10 percent glycolic lotion with a pH of 3.5 or higher should be applied on damp skin once or twice a day. This combination of alpha hydroxy exfoliation and light hydration on a daily basis helps reduce the roughness and redness associated with this condition.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 86)

title: Menarche, Menstruation, and the Menstrual Cycle
content: With puberty and adolescence comes **menarche**, which is a girl’s first "period." **Menstruation** is the correct name for the period, and it is actually the body getting rid of an unused ovum and accompanying endometrium (the lining of the uterine wall), a process that occurs each month in females. The endometrium is formed by the uterus in response to the hormone progesterone. The menstrual cycle is a 27- to 30-day cycle in which the female’s ovaries manufacture an ovum and hormonal changes take place that prepare the uterus for pregnancy in case the ovum is fertilized by sperm. When the ovum is not fertilized, the uterus sheds the lining (endometrium), which is menstruation (as shown in Figure 5-5). The menstrual cycle actually involves six phases. Phase 1 occurs during the first five days of the cycle. The hypothalamus senses low levels of estrogen and progesterone and signals the pituitary gland to secrete Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) and Luteinizing Hormone (LH). The ovaries then begin producing larger amounts of estrogen and progesterone, which prepare and release the ovum.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 86)

title: Melasma
content: Melasma refers to splotchy, pigmented complexions that can manifest on the skin. This condition is often associated with hormonal changes, such as those occurring after the use of birth control pills.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 87)

title: The Placenta and its Hormonal Influence on Skin During Pregnancy
content: The trophoblast, initially a capsule-like structure providing nutrition to the embryo, evolves into the placenta. The placenta is a thick layer of hormone-producing cells that functions as the crucial nutrient, oxygen, and waste-exchange system between the growing embryo and the mother's blood system. It manufactures numerous hormones that influence various bodily processes, including effects on the corpus luteum, embryo growth, and milk production in the mother’s breasts. These hormones are responsible for many of the physiological and skin changes observed during pregnancy.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 88)

title: Hyperpigmentation and Melasma (Pregnancy Mask) During Pregnancy
content:
Hyperpigmentation is a common skin change during pregnancy, often manifesting as skin that tans much more easily than normal. A specific form is the 'pregnancy mask,' or melasma (Figures 5-6 and 5-7), a condition where the face develops significant hyperpigmentation, appearing as a dark facial mask. Typically, light areas surround the eyes, with the rest of the face appearing dark with pigment.

**Cause:** Both pregnancy masks and general pregnancy-related hyperpigmentation are caused by a hormone secreted by the placenta that stimulates melanocytes in the skin.

**Management and Treatment:**
*   Normally, this hyperpigmentation subsides after childbirth.
*   If it does not fade within several months post-childbirth, it can then be treated in a salon or by a professional.
*   Treating hyperpigmentation *during* pregnancy is not advisable. Even if skin bleaches like hydroquinone could achieve a lightening effect, the placenta continues to produce the melanin-stimulating hormone, leading to further hyperpigmentation.
*   Avoiding sun exposure significantly helps reduce the amount of pigment produced during pregnancy.
*   It is best to wait until after pregnancy to assess if the hyperpigmentation decreases with the shutdown of placental hormones.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Pages 88-89)

title: Telangiectasias and Couperose During Pregnancy
content: Telangiectasias are small, red, enlarged capillaries that can appear on the face and other areas of the body. Couperose refers to diffuse redness, which often includes telangiectasias, on the face and other body areas. These conditions can become more apparent during pregnancy. It is recommended to wait until after pregnancy to observe if these conditions improve before considering treatment.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 89)

title: Stretch Marks (Striae) During Pregnancy
content: Stretch marks, also known as striae (Figure 5-8), are common marks that occur in pregnant women. These strip-like lines vary in appearance from red or brown to tan. They are a direct result of rapid weight gain during pregnancy, which causes the skin to stretch quickly. Common areas for stretch marks include the abdomen, breasts, buttocks, and legs. Unfortunately, there is generally little that can be done to completely eliminate stretch marks, although many tend to fade after childbirth.
sourceReference: Skin Care: Beyond the Basics (4th Edition) - Chapter 5: Hormones (Page 89)
